title: Jars of Clay
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro

subpoints: A. The treasure is 'the light of the knowledge of the glory of God in the face of Jesus Christ' — the gospel with its full personal and existential weight
B. The Dead Sea Scrolls as an illustration: priceless contents in common clay jars
C. God intentionally places priceless treasure into weak, fragile, replaceable vessels — primarily ministers of the gospel
D. God's purpose: that no one mistakes where the power comes from — the excellence of the power is of God, not of us
A. Four paradoxes of Christian ministry: hard pressed but not crushed; perplexed but not in despair; persecuted but not forsaken; struck down but not destroyed
B. Paul's catalogue of sufferings: five times 39 lashes, three times beaten with rods, once stoned, three shipwrecks, imprisoned (2 Corinthians 11:22-33)
C. God does not remove suffering but preserves his servants through it — sustaining grace in the midst of pressure
D. 2 Corinthians 12:9: 'My grace is sufficient for you, for My strength is made perfect in weakness'
A. 'Always carrying about in the body the dying of the Lord Jesus, that the life of Jesus also may be manifested in our body'
B. Paul follows the example of Christ who laid down his life for the sheep — he lays down his life for the Corinthians
C. Death works in Paul (suffering) so that life works in the Corinthians (the gospel reaches them)
D. The resurrection as motivation: Paul endures because he knows there is a bodily life to come

outline.point: IV. Four Ways to Live as Jars of Clay
subpoints: A. Embrace your weakness instead of hiding it — our weakness is the stage on which God's power is displayed
B. In difficulty, remember: hard pressed does not mean crushed; God promises sustaining grace
C. Die to self in the small daily moments — choosing patience over anger, serving instead of demanding, forgiving instead of grudging
D. Intentionally pour the treasure of the gospel into others — discipling, evangelizing, encouraging, teaching children

summary:
Preaching from 2 Corinthians 4:1-12 on March 15, 2026, Pastor Lyro opens with the image of the Dead Sea Scrolls — priceless biblical manuscripts discovered in common clay jars in a cave near the Dead Sea in 1947, where they had been hidden for 2,000 years. The jar itself was worthless; the contents were priceless. This is exactly Paul's picture in verse 7: the gospel — 'the light of the knowledge of the glory of God in the face of Jesus Christ' — is placed by God in the fragile, ordinary, easily-broken vessels of human beings, especially ministers of the gospel. God's intentional purpose is that no one mistakes the source of the power: the excellence belongs entirely to God.

The sermon traces three movements in the passage: the treasure we carry (v. 7), the pressure we endure (vv. 8-9), and the life we display (vv. 10-12). The four paradoxes of verses 8-9 — hard pressed but not crushed, perplexed but not in despair, persecuted but not forsaken, struck down but not destroyed — are set against the backdrop of Paul's extraordinary personal suffering catalogued in 2 Corinthians 11. The pressure is real; but so is God's preserving grace. The sermon closes with four practical applications: embrace weakness instead of hiding it; trust God's sustaining grace in difficulty; die to self in ordinary daily moments; and intentionally pour the treasure of the gospel into others through evangelism, discipleship, and the teaching of children. The contrast between a dying star (supernova) that pours its energy into the universe and a black hole that implodes into itself serves as the closing illustration of the two kinds of lives available to those who carry Christ.
transcript:
## Scripture Reading: 2 Corinthians 4:1-12

If you're able to remain standing for our scripture reading, we're turning to 2 Corinthians chapter 4. We're reading verses 1 through 12 of 2 Corinthians chapter 4.

Therefore, since we have this ministry, as we have received mercy, we do not lose heart. But we have renounced the hidden things of shame, not walking in craftiness nor handling the word of God deceitfully, but by manifestation of the truth commending ourselves to every man's conscience in the sight of God. But even if our gospel is veiled, it is veiled to those who are perishing, whose minds the god of this age has blinded, who do not believe, lest the light of the gospel of the glory of Christ, who is the image of God, should shine on them. For we do not preach ourselves, but Christ Jesus the Lord, and ourselves your bondservants for Jesus' sake. For it is the God who commanded light to shine out of darkness, who has shone in our hearts to give the light of the knowledge of the glory of God in the face of Jesus Christ. But we have this treasure in earthen vessels, that the excellence of the power may be of God and not of us. We are hard-pressed on every side, yet not crushed; we are perplexed, but not in despair; persecuted, but not forsaken; struck down, but not destroyed — always carrying about in the body the dying of the Lord Jesus, that the life of Jesus also may be manifested in our body. For we who live are always delivered to death for Jesus' sake, that the life of Jesus also may be manifested in our mortal flesh. So then death is working in us, but life in you.

This is the word of our Lord. Let us pray. Father in heaven, thank you that you speak — we pray you speak to us through this passage. We pray, Father, that your Spirit would use this mightily for our good, for your glory. We ask in Jesus' name, amen. Please be seated.

## Introduction: The Dead Sea Scrolls

In the ancient world, people often stored valuable things in very ordinary clay jars. These jars were cheap, common, and easily broken. There is nothing important about those jars. Archaeologists have found them all over the Mediterranean world — they exist in abundance, they are not rare, they don't command a great price. They were the ancient equivalent of a paper bag or a cardboard box — you just put things you wanted to store inside them. The jar itself was not admired by anyone. But sometimes the contents were extremely valuable: money, legal documents, scrolls at times — priceless things stored inside simple containers.

One famous modern example: in 1947, a Bedouin shepherd happened to enter a cave near the Dead Sea in southern Israel. He found some jars, and in those jars he found some scrolls — the scroll kind, not the bushy-tail kind. When scholars examined them, they saw these were ancient manuscripts of the Bible that had been hidden in these common everyday jars for 2,000 years. Those manuscripts became what we know as the Dead Sea Scrolls. They advanced biblical scholarship by leaps and bounds, pushing the oldest known Hebrew manuscript from the 10th century back to the 2nd century BC — and proving that the Bible we have today is the same Bible they had then. The jars didn't really matter. The text inside was priceless.

That is exactly what Paul pictures here in 2 Corinthians 4:7. The gospel — the knowledge of the glory of God in the face of Jesus Christ — is the treasure. God places that treasure in fragile human containers intentionally. He puts priceless treasure into weak vessels so that no one mistakes where the power comes from. The power is not in the jar. The power is in the content: the gospel of Jesus Christ.

That is the point Paul unfolds in this passage. God displays his power through weak, suffering servants so that the light of Christ may be seen.

I want to look at three things: the treasure we carry (verse 7), the pressure we endure (verses 8-9), and the life we display (verses 10-12).

## I. The Treasure We Carry (v. 7)

Look at verse 7: "But we have this treasure in earthen vessels."

This treasure is the light of the knowledge of the glory of God in the face of Jesus Christ — mentioned in verse 6. It is the gospel revealed to us in Jesus Christ. But it is more than just the bare facts of the gospel. This is not merely: God's Son became flesh, lived perfectly, died the death we deserve, rose again, ascended, and is seated at the right hand of the Father. This is all of that — but more. It is the *knowledge of the glory* of God in the face of Jesus Christ: the personal, emotional, relational, transforming encounter with what the gospel means. It is more precious than the most precious thing you can imagine.

As Jesus himself said in Matthew 13:44: "The kingdom of heaven is like treasure hidden in a field, which a man found and hid; and for joy over it he goes and sells all that he has and buys that field."

And this great treasure — more precious than anything imaginable — is carried around in ordinary, fragile, cheap clay jars. When Paul uses the pronouns "we" and "our," he's primarily talking about himself and Timothy (see 1:1). Paul is teaching that the gospel is carried around in common, cheap, easily-broken jars of clay. We call those pastors.

Paul is well aware of the fragility of life after suffering greatly for the sake of Christ. He can feel death in his bones every morning when he gets up and feels the beating he took in Ephesus, or the shipwreck off the coast of Cilicia, or the caning he got in Antioch. He is very aware that there is nothing special about him that makes his message powerful.

You know what the Bible calls a minister who realizes his message is powerful because of the one he's proclaiming, not because of anything in himself? A jar of clay. Fragile. Ordinary. Disposable. And you know what the Bible calls a minister who thinks the success of the gospel depends on his gifts? A whitewashed sepulcher — a tomb. In one there is the message of life; in the other, only the stench of death.

Now, the imagery of jars of clay primarily refers to the minister of the gospel, but we can extend this metaphor to apply to every Christian. The power of our message is not in the beauty of the vessel — it is in the content that vessel carries.

Our bodies are decaying. We are clay pots, brothers and sisters. A dime a dozen. Paul Barnett in his commentary says: "Human life is short, its form easily defaced, its fabric destructible in a second. It is an earthen jar, a cheap clay pot." That's who we are. But our inner self is being renewed day by day because we carry in us the light of the knowledge of the glory of God in the face of Jesus Christ.

Why does God do things this way? Why use fragile, replaceable, not-at-all-special people to carry his gospel? Paul tells us: "that the excellence of the power may be of God and not of us." Clay jars display the power of God best.

As a side note: you want to do great things for God? Recognize your weakness. Because God will not use the person who is strong in himself. He uses the weak who know that all their sufficiency is in Christ Jesus. As Jesus told Paul in 2 Corinthians 12:9: "My grace is sufficient for you, for My strength is made perfect in weakness."

And clay pots don't call attention to themselves. Those jars sat in that Dead Sea cave for 2,000 years before anyone thought to look inside them. Yet people know there might be something worth waiting around to see. You have the gospel of Jesus Christ. No one attributes the success to the messenger — the success is in the power of the gospel in the clay pot.

And the purpose of the jar is not only to store but to *pour*. To pour out in evangelism. To pour out in discipleship. To pour out in righteous living for the glory of God.

Before we move on, take a moment right now to think. Do people get a true sense of Christ when they are around you? What are you pouring out of your jar?

## II. The Pressure We Endure (vv. 8-9)

After talking about the treasure we carry, Paul turns to the pressure we endure because of the treasure we carry. Verses 8-9:

"We are hard-pressed on every side, yet not crushed; we are perplexed, but not in despair; persecuted, but not forsaken; struck down, but not destroyed."

Four paradoxes describing Christian ministry. Hard pressed but not crushed. Perplexed but not in despair. Persecuted but not forsaken. Struck down but not destroyed. We see the hard in the first element and the doable-by-the-grace-of-God in the second.

If Paul were posting a job description, he would not get many applicants. Pastor wanted: to be hard pressed, perplexed, persecuted, and struck down. Low pay. And yet this is the reality of gospel ministry.

Paul carried in his body all the struggles associated with ministering the gospel. Look at chapter 1:8-10: "We were burdened beyond measure, above strength, so that we despaired even of life. Yes, we had the sentence of death in ourselves, that we should not trust in ourselves but in God who raises the dead, who delivered us from so great a death, and does deliver us." And in chapter 6:3-10: "in tribulations, in needs, in distresses, in stripes, in imprisonments, in tumults, in labors, in sleeplessness, in fasting... as dying, and behold we live; as chastened, and yet not killed; as sorrowful, yet always rejoicing; as poor, yet making many rich; as having nothing, and yet possessing all things."

And then there is the longer list in chapter 11. Five times he received 39 lashes from the Jews — whipped 195 times. Three times beaten with rods. Once stoned. Three times shipwrecked. A night and a day in the deep. Journeys often, in perils of rivers, robbers, his own countrymen, Gentiles, the city, the wilderness, the sea, false brethren. Weariness, toil, sleeplessness often, hunger, thirst, cold, nakedness. And he suffered even more after writing this letter — at least one more shipwreck, three more imprisonments.

Yet he says: we are hard pressed but not crushed. Perplexed but not in despair. Persecuted but not forsaken. Struck down but not destroyed. Why not? Because of the surpassing power of God that is in us.

Gospel ministry and gospel living have their challenges. But God sustains us in and through them. God does not remove suffering — he preserves his servants through it.

## III. The Life We Display (vv. 10-12)

Finally, Paul points us to the life we display. Verses 10-12:

"Always carrying about in the body the dying of the Lord Jesus, that the life of Jesus also may be manifested in our body. For we who live are always delivered to death for Jesus' sake, that the life of Jesus also may be manifested in our mortal flesh. So then death is working in us, but life in you."

Carrying in the body "the dying of the Lord Jesus" is Paul's way of speaking about the physical and emotional pain he has gone through because of the ministry of the new covenant. Paul is beaten up physically and emotionally because of the proclamation of the gospel. But in that, the life of Jesus is manifested in his body — and will be fully manifested in the resurrection of the righteous at the return of Christ.

What we see here is Paul following the example of his Lord, who laid down his life for the sheep. Paul lays his life down for the flock God has entrusted to him. He's not saving the Corinthians by dying for them — but he is sacrificing himself for their sake.

Brothers and sisters, we are jars of clay filled with Christ himself. If suffering or dying is what it takes for us to pour Christ out, praise be to God. Very few of us will be called to die physically for Christ. But we are all called to die to self in the service of one another.

As Paul says in Philippians 2:3-4: "Let nothing be done through selfish ambition or conceit, but in lowliness of mind let each esteem others better than himself, each of you looking out not only for his own interests, but also for the interests of others."

How is your dying going? Are you dying to self as you pour the treasure of Christ into those around you?

Our Lord himself said: "If anyone desires to come after Me, let him deny himself, and take up his cross, and follow Me. For whoever desires to save his life will lose it, but whoever loses his life for My sake will find it."

Paul's motivation: all his suffering, all this dying in his body, brings life to the Corinthians. "Death is working in us, but life in you" — not atoning death like Christ's, but the willingness to take the gospel to people at personal cost. Paul's death equals their life because Paul is willing to do that.

And Paul is also motivated by the hope of the resurrection. Why waste away in this life? Because there is a life to come in the body. He will be raised again to life. He will be like the Lord Jesus Christ. This life was not his best life now — and he knew it, and it kept him going.

## IV. Four Ways to Live as Jars of Clay

We don't want to be people who just hear — we want to be people who do what we hear. Four applications:

**First: Embrace your weakness instead of hiding it.** Many people feel disqualified from serving God because they feel inadequate, ordinary, broken. But that is exactly the kind of vessel God uses. We are not to wait until we have our sins in order, not wait until we're stronger, not wait until we're not broken. Our weakness is not an obstacle to God's work — it's often the stage on which his power is displayed.

**Second: In difficulty, remember that hard pressed does not mean crushed.** Do not assume something is wrong when life becomes hard because you're following Christ. The Christian life may include pressure, but God's promise is sustaining grace in the midst of it. Don't measure your usefulness to God by how comfortable you are. This book starts by calling God the "God of all comfort who comforts us in all our tribulations" — and then spends most of the letter describing what that comfort looks like in the context of suffering.

**Third: Die to self in the small daily moments.** We will likely not be called to die as martyrs. But we are called to die to ourselves in ordinary situations: choosing patience instead of anger, serving instead of demanding to be served, forgiving instead of holding grudges, putting the needs of others before our own. Every act of self-denial becomes a way of pouring the life of Christ into others.

**Fourth: Intentionally pour the treasure of the gospel into others.** The jar is not only meant to hold — it is meant to pour. Ask yourself regularly: who am I investing in spiritually? This may look like sharing the gospel with a neighbor, discipling a younger believer, encouraging a struggling friend, or teaching your children the Word of God. God did not place the treasure of Christ in you merely to store it — but to pour it into the lives of others.

In Sunday school today, one of two things happened: you either got a really good nap, or you learned some things about physics and we talked about a supernova — a star that died but all its energy was poured out into the universe. That's really the Christian dying to self — everything poured into the universe, and everything is illuminated.

But there's another kind of dying star. They're not called supernovas — they're called black holes. As a star dies, instead of pouring outward, it implodes into itself. It focuses on itself and drags everything around it into its darkness. And the only thing you can see regarding those stars is darkness.

So who are you? Are you a supernova — dying to self, and yet your energy blasted out over all creation? Or are you a black hole — sucking everybody else dry, and all that can be seen is darkness?

Paul says: you are a jar of clay — replaceable, common, fragile. Yet you bear in you the greatest treasure: Jesus Christ himself. Let us pour him out as we go.

Let's pray. Father, thank you that you are a powerful God in the lives of the weak. We pray that in our weakness you would show yourself strong. We ask in Jesus' name, amen.
